title: Metastable MnBi$_2$Te$_4$ enabled by magnetic-field-assisted synthesis
authors: Abhinna Rajbanshi, G. M. Zills, Alexander M. Donald, Daniel Duong, David Graf, James J. Hamlin, Mark W. Meisel, I. Vekhter, Williams A. Shelton, Rongying Jin
abstract: Magnetic topological insulators provide a unique platform to explore the interplay between magnetism and topology. MnBi$_2$Te$_4$, known for its A-type antiferromagnetic (AFM) ground state, undergoes a striking transformation when single crystals are grown in an applied magnetic field. Despite retaining the same crystal structure, field-grown MnBi$_2$Te$_4$ exhibits a ferromagnetic (FM) ground state with a Curie temperature of $\sim$ 12.5 K, confirmed by magnetization, magnetic torque, electrical resistivity, and specific heat measurements. First-principles calculations support these findings, revealing that magnetic-field-assisted synthesis can effectively reconfigure the ground-state spin order and thereby modify the material's electronic properties, as reflected in the de Haas-van Alphen oscillation seen in the magnetic torque.
summary: This paper demonstrates that growing MnBi2Te4 in a magnetic field allows for the creation of a metastable ferromagnetic state with a different electronic structure than the standard antiferromagnetic version. This method provides a new way to tune the topological properties and magnetic phases of intrinsic magnetic topological insulators.
main_problem: The study aims to find ways to control and reconfigure the magnetic ordering and electronic properties of the magnetic topological insulator MnBi2Te4 beyond its native A-type antiferromagnetic state.
main_result: By using magnetic-field-assisted synthesis, the researchers successfully stabilized a metastable ferromagnetic ground state in MnBi2Te4, which exhibits a different Fermi surface and potential type-II Weyl semimetal phase.
method: The researchers used a self-flux growth method in a 9 T magnetic field and characterized the resulting crystals using X-ray diffraction, magnetometry (VSM, torque), transport measurements (resistivity, Hall effect), and first-principles DFT calculations.
model_or_system: The physical system is MnBi2Te4, a layered van der Waals intrinsic magnetic topological insulator. The study compares the native A-type antiferromagnetic (AFM) state with a field-induced metastable ferromagnetic (FM) state.
key_observables: Magnetic susceptibility, magnetization, magnetic torque, electrical resistivity, Hall resistivity, de Haas-van Alphen oscillations, and specific heat.
important_parameters: Curie temperature (Tc approx 12.5 K), magnetic field strength (up to 35 T), and the transition from S=5/2 to S=1/2 spin state.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the observed changes in electronic properties are primarily driven by the reconfiguration of the magnetic ground state and orbital contributions rather than spin scattering.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the crystal structure, XRD patterns, and peak shifts; Figure 2 displays temperature-dependent magnetic susceptibility; Figure 3 presents resistivity and Hall effect data.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of magnetic control, describes the field-assisted synthesis method, presents structural and magnetic characterization of the new phase, details the transport and quantum oscillation measurements, and concludes with DFT-supported analysis of the electronic structure.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. (a) Temperature dependence of the magnetic susceptibility of field-grown MnBi2Te4 with the magnetic field H 1000 Oe applied along c axis (χc) and within ab plane Type equation here. (χab). (b) Comparison of experimental χc and χab with the Curie-Weiss fit results represented by solid curves. (c) Derivative of the magnetic susceptibility, indicating Tx and TC. (d-f) Field dependence of the magnetization at 2 K (d), 10 K (e) and 15 K (f). Insets are zoomed-in magnetization for both the c and ab directions. (g) Temperature dependence of the specific heat. The red line represents the fitting of data with Eq. (1). Inset: low-temperature specific heat plotted as Cp/T vs. T2. (h)...

FIG. 3. (a) Temperature dependence of the in-plane (ρab) and out-of-plane (ρc) of field-grown MnBi2Te4. (b) Low-temperature ρab at different magnetic fields applied along the c axis plotted as ρab vs. T2. (c) Magnetic field dependence of MRab with H // c (θ 0∘) at different temperatures. Inset: MR between -3 T and 3 T. (d) Field dependence of MR at T 0.4 K with the magnetic field direction varying between the c and ab directions. (e) Magnetic field dependence of the Hall resistivity (ρxy) at different temperatures with H // c. (f) Comparison of ρxy for zero-field-grown (green), field-annealed (red) and field-grown (black) MnBi2Te4 at T 2 K.

FIG. 4. (a-b) Field dependence of the magnetic torque for zero-field-grown and field-grown MnBi2Te4 with magnetic field applied along the c axis (θ 0∘) (a) and (b) ab plane (θ 90°). (c-d) Field dependence of the magnetic torque for θ 15∘, 30∘, 45∘, 60∘, 75∘ for (c) field- grown and (d) zero-field-grown MnBi2Te4.

FIG. 5. (a) Magnetic field dependence of the magnetic torque (τ) of field-grown MnBi2Te4 at T 0.4 K with H // c between 8 T and 35 T. The red curve represents the background τbg. (b) τ versus 1/H. (c) FFT of ∆τ revealing a peak at F ~ 40 T. (d) Landau fan diagram constructed from the dHvA oscillation.
FIG. 6. Band structures for MnBi2Te4 with A-type AFM (a) and FM (b) ground states.
